activity:
6:00 AM – Morning Inspection of Slaughterhouse Floor  
Before the sun rises over the Buenos Aires meatpacking district, the manager arrives at the plant to conduct a quiet, solitary inspection of the slaughterhouse floor. He walks the cold, blood-slicked concrete, checking for proper drainage, cleanliness of the hook systems, and the condition of the stunning pens. He notes the time each steer enters the stunning chamber and observes the precision of the workers’ cuts—his decades of experience allow him to detect inefficiencies in the chain of slaughter that others overlook. He carries a leather-bound logbook, in which he records observations on throughput, waste, and any deviations from standard procedure. He pauses to speak with the night-shift foreman, asking about any injuries or mechanical failures during the shift, and makes a mental note to review the steam-powered conveyor belt’s alignment later that day.

7:30 AM – Breakfast with the Foremen and Union Representatives  
In the modest but clean break room, he joins a small group of foremen and two elected worker representatives from the newly formed Meat Packers’ Mutual Aid Society. Over steaming bowls of *choclo con queso* and strong *mate*, he listens to their concerns: the lack of gloves for handling offal, the rising cost of boots due to import tariffs, and the need for better ventilation in the tallow rendering room. He doesn’t just nod—he takes notes, and promises to petition the company board for improved PPE and a small grant to install a new exhaust fan. He also shares a recent article from *La Nación* about the Chicago Packing House Strike of 1904, drawing parallels to their own working conditions, and encourages dialogue over confrontation.
